_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. Tachograph hardware installation and certification**
All in-scope LCVs (2.5–3.5t used in cross-border commercial freight or cabotage) must be fitted with a second-generation smart tachograph (G2V2 / Smart Tachograph Version 2, e.g. VDO DTCO 4.1a or equivalent). Installation must be performed by a certified specialist workshop, which calibrates the device and applies a company lock. The workspace must identify every qualifying vehicle operated directly or sub-contracted for European road legs and confirm retrofit bookings are secured before the 1 July 2026 deadline. Workshop capacity is already constrained; IRU and multiple industry sources report that last-minute demand leads to device shortages and multi-week queues. Gap from baseline: most LCV operators — and workspace subcontractors in the live events, fine art, luxury, and film verticals accustomed to van-based final-mile delivery — will never have managed tachograph procurement. This is a net-new operational requirement.

**2. Tachograph data management platform**
Installing the device is necessary but not sufficient. The workspace must procure or configure a tachograph data management system capable of: (a) downloading driver card data at least every 28 calendar days; (b) downloading vehicle unit data at least every 90 calendar days; (c) archiving all data securely with protection against unauthorised access for a minimum of 12 months; and (d) maintaining 56 days of inspection-ready data accessible at any roadside check. Remote download functionality (over-the-air via G2V2 capability) is technically possible and recommended to reduce administrative burden. Gap from baseline: the workspace likely has no existing tachograph data infrastructure for LCV operations. New investment or SaaS platform procurement is required.

**3. Driver hours scheduling and route planning system updates**
Operational route planning for all European LCV movements must be updated to enforce: 9-hour daily driving limit (10 hours maximum twice per week); 56-hour weekly limit; 90-hour two-week limit; mandatory 45-minute break after 4.5 hours of driving. Dispatchers and subcontractor managers must be instructed not to set schedules that require drivers to violate these limits. For time-critical cargo verticals (live events, film and TV, automotive reveals), this means scheduling buffer time for mandatory breaks and rest periods must be factored into transit time commitments and client SLA agreements. Gap from baseline: current LCV dispatch instructions for these verticals typically assume flexible van driver scheduling with no regulated break structure.

**4. Driver training**
LCV drivers hold only a Category B licence and have had no structured exposure to EU transport regulations. The workspace must ensure that all drivers operating qualifying vehicles — whether directly employed or engaged through subcontractors — receive training on: tachograph device operation and country symbol entry; driving and rest time limits and daily logging; posting-of-drivers rules and document-carriage requirements; and procedures for roadside inspections. IRU's Academy offers training programmes on these topics. Subcontractor contracts should require evidence of driver training completion. Gap from baseline: EU Regulation 561/2006 training is entirely new for this driver population.

**5. Supplier and subcontractor onboarding and contract clauses**
The workspace must insert the following into all road haulage subcontractor agreements covering EU LCV movements: warranty that the carrier holds a valid Community licence with certified copy on board; warranty that all in-scope vehicles are fitted with a calibrated G2V2 tachograph; warranty that posting declarations will be filed via RTPD/IMI prior to each qualifying operation; obligation to provide tachograph download records on request; right of the workspace to audit compliance status. Scope: all active European subcontractors using vans for cross-border moves, effective 1 July 2026.

**6. Internal compliance monitoring and audit**
The workspace should establish a periodic audit cycle — at minimum quarterly for the first year post-implementation — to verify that subcontractors maintain tachograph calibration currency, data download cadence, and posting declaration filing. Non-compliant vehicles can be immobilised at borders, which for live events, film, and art transport would constitute a critical service failure. Gap from baseline: no equivalent monitoring obligation currently exists for LCV subcontractors operating below the 3.5t threshold.

*Source: IRU, "EU cross-border transport rules extend to vans: what you need to know", IRU, May 2026. https://www.iru.org/news-resources/newsroom/eu-cross-border-transport-rules-extend-vans-what-you-need-know; AUMOVIO, "New EU requirement for light commercial vehicles", 2025. https://www.aumovio.com/en/company/fairs-and-events/busworld-2025/media-information-tachographenpflicht.html; Queclink, "EU Mobility Package and Tachograph Requirements", May 2026. https://www.queclink.com/2026/05/13/eu-mobility-package-and-tachograph-requirements-what-fleet-operators-need-to-know-in-2026/*
